metadata.summary: Design the API in OpenAPI before code, generate stubs and SDKs, validate implementation conforms.

content.title: API Contract-First Development
content.summary: Write the OpenAPI spec before any implementation code, get it reviewed like a PR, then generate server stubs and client SDKs from it. The spec is the source of truth; CI validates that the running implementation still matches it. Breaking changes require spec version bumps.

why.p: Designing the API surface in YAML forces deliberate decisions about resource shapes, error responses, and versioning before any code exists — reversing those decisions is cheap at spec stage, expensive after clients integrate. Code generation eliminates drift between spec and implementation. Spectral linting catches naming and structure violations automatically.

list.item: Starting a new API that will have external consumers or multiple client teams.
Adding a significant new resource or workflow to an existing API.
Integrating with a partner that requires a published contract.
Any API where mobile and backend teams work in parallel.
Internal APIs consumed only by one service written by the same team.
Rapid prototyping where the API shape is still being discovered.
APIs that are wrappers over a third-party spec (just import their spec).
Micro-services that communicate exclusively over gRPC/protobuf.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A four-stage pipeline. (1) Design: a domain-modeling agent produces an OpenAPI 3.1 spec from PRD + REST style guide; a critic agent runs Spectral and the api-rest-design rules. (2) Approve: human-in-the-loop merge gate; oasdiff breaking blocks regressions. (3) Generate: CI pipeline emits server stubs (FastAPI / NestJS / go-chi), client SDKs (TS, Py, Go), and contract test scaffolds via openapi-generator or orval/oazapfts. (4) Implement: an SDD-driven coding agent fills business logic in the generated stub, never editing the generated models — contract tests enforce the spec. Repeat: any spec change re-runs (3) and any new endpoint re-runs (1)–(4).

rule.statement: Each endpoint life-cycle requires human approval at the spec stage (design review gate) before code generation begins.
rule.rationale: Agents can produce technically valid specs that are semantically poor. Human-in-loop ensures the contract aligns with product intent before consuming teams depend on stubs.

example.title: Four-stage spec-first pipeline
example.code:
Stage 1 — Design: - Agent reads PRD and api-rest-design rules - Produces openapi.yaml with all endpoints, request/response schemas, error definitions, examples - Runs Spectral; outputs PASS/WARN/FAIL
Stage 2 — Approve: - Human reviews spec PR, checks resource naming, HTTP methods, status codes - Runs oasdiff breaking; blocks unless MAJOR version bump
Stage 3 — Generate: - CI runs openapi-generator → FastAPI server stubs, TypeScript client, contract test scaffolds - All generated code committed (marked DO NOT EDIT)
Stage 4 — Implement: - Agent fills NotImplementedError in server; forbidden from editing models - Runs pytest -k contract_<operationId> per endpoint - Commits implementation code only

section.name: workflow
section.p: Six-phase workflow from spec design to deployment, with CI integration rules.

rule.statement: The OpenAPI spec is the source of truth. Implementation must conform to the spec; the spec is never updated to match a drifted implementation.
rule.rationale: Allowing spec-to-implementation drift invalidates generated clients and documentation. Once consumers rely on the spec, breaking it silently causes integration failures.

rule.statement: Treat spec changes that break existing responses (remove fields, change types) as breaking changes requiring a version bump.
rule.rationale: Additive changes (new optional fields) are backwards-compatible. Removals and type changes break existing clients silently.

rule.statement: Run Spectral lint on every PR that touches the spec file; fail the build on errors.
rule.rationale: Spectral catches missing operationIds, undocumented error responses, and naming violations before review — issues that are cheap to fix early and expensive later.

rule.statement: Run contract tests (openapi-core response validation) on every commit; do not merge if responses diverge from the spec.
rule.rationale: Contract tests are the only automated check that the running implementation still matches the published spec.

rule.statement: Generate server stubs and client SDKs from the spec using openapi-generator; regenerate on every spec change.
rule.rationale: Manual synchronization between spec and generated code always drifts. Automated regeneration is the only reliable approach.

example.title: openapi-generator commands
example.code:
# Generate Python FastAPI server stubs openapi-generator generate \ -i openapi.yaml \ -g python-fastapi \ -o ./server \ --additional-properties=packageName=payment_api
# Generate TypeScript Axios client openapi-generator generate \ -i openapi.yaml \ -g typescript-axios \ -o ./client

rule.statement: Enforce at minimum: operation-operationId (error), oas3-schema (error), operation-description (warn).
rule.rationale: Missing operationIds break SDK generation. Schema errors break client validation. Missing descriptions degrade documentation quality.

example.title: Spectral configuration
example.code: # .spectral.yaml extends: spectral:oas rules: operation-operationId: error operation-description: warn info-contact: warn oas3-schema: error

section.name: contract-validation
section.p: Validate that running responses match the spec using openapi-core and schemathesis.

example.title: Python contract test

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: Contract-first development relies on a suite of CI/CD tooling and SaaS platforms. Select tools based on language support, IDE integration, and team size. Core tools: openapi-generator (code generation), oapi-codegen (Go), orval (TypeScript), spectral (spec linting), oasdiff (breaking-change detection), schemathesis (property-based testing), buf (gRPC equivalent). SaaS platforms include Stoplight (visual design + governance), SwaggerHub (hosted governance), Bump.sh (versioning + breaking-change diffs), Apicurio Registry (self-hosted registry), Postman (spec import + contract tests), Speakeasy (polished SDK generation), Fern (SDK + docs), Buf Schema Registry (gRPC registry), and Konnect (Kong gateway).

section.name: failures-and-limitations
section.p: Spec drift: developers patch the controller and forget the spec; without CI enforcement the spec rots quickly. Generated code ergonomics: openapi-generator output is often verbose, idiomatic-poor, and locked to template choices. Generator bugs: certain Python / TypeScript generators emit broken code for oneOf, recursive schemas, or additionalProperties — silently. Breaking-change detection is brittle: same JSON schema can be expressed two ways and oasdiff reports false breaks. Conditional / polymorphic responses (oneOf, anyOf, discriminator) are hard for generators and harder for SDK consumers. AsyncAPI tooling lags OpenAPI by 2–3 years; stub generation is shaky for event-driven contracts. Spec authoring tools (Stoplight Studio, Swagger Editor) make easy contracts easy and hard contracts harder; agents often produce spec that is technically valid but semantically poor. Mock servers diverge from real backend semantics (auth, idempotency, rate limits) — clients pass mock tests and break in staging.

section.name: agentic-gotchas
section.p: Agents edit generated files when "fixing tests". Mark generated dirs with // CODEGEN — DO NOT EDIT headers and add a pre-commit hook that fails on changes inside them. LLMs invent OpenAPI fields that don't exist (x-magic-flag) or use deprecated 2.0 syntax. Pin the prompt to OAS 3.1 and validate with the official JSON Schema. Polymorphic schemas (oneOf / discriminator) trip both generators and agents — keep schemas flat where possible and document the discriminator value. Agents skip examples to save tokens. Force examples blocks on every request and 2xx response via Spectral oas3-valid-media-example plus a custom "min-1-example" rule. Agents conflate "spec" and "docs" — they'll add narrative prose into description fields. Keep description short; reference external docs via externalDocs. Breaking-change blindness: agents widen response shapes (add a null to a non-nullable field) without bumping major. oasdiff catches this; the agent prompt must be told to read its output. Mock-server overconfidence: agents wire frontend to Prism mock and declare "done"; mock semantics differ from real (auth, rate limits). Require contract tests against the real server before completing an SDD task. Human-in-loop checkpoints: any spec PR labeled breaking must be human-approved; any new resource must pass design review before code generation runs. Generator pinning: agents may bump generator versions silently; pin the version in package.json / Makefile and treat upgrades as their own PR.

section.name: recommended-agents-and-subagents
section.p: Recommended subagents for contract-first pipelines: faion-sdd-executor-agent (gates: spec compiles, Spectral clean, oasdiff non-breaking or major-bumped, generated code committed in same PR). A spec-critic subagent (worth creating): runs Spectral + custom rules from api-rest-design and api-versioning; outputs PASS/FAIL with rule IDs. /faion (skill) — sequences the four stages as ordered tasks; pauses at the human-approve gate. A stub-impl subagent: instructed to fill NotImplementedError in the generated server with business logic, forbidden from touching the model files. password-scrubber-agent — scrub examples in spec before publishing.

section.name: prompt-pattern-for-spec-drafting
section.code: You are an API designer. Produce an OpenAPI 3.1 YAML for the use cases in <prd>. Follow Zalando RESTful guidelines and the rules in api-rest-design/README.md. Use Problem+JSON for errors. Tag every operation. Include 2 examples per request and per 2xx response. Output only the YAML; no commentary.

section.name: prompt-pattern-for-stub-fill
section.code: You are filling generated server stubs. Files under server/models/** are GENERATED — DO NOT EDIT. Implement only methods raising NotImplementedError in server/apis/**. Use the repository in server/repositories/<resource>.py. After each method, run pytest -k contract_<operationId>; the test must pass.

section.name: cli-tools-ecosystem
section.p: Contract-first development uses a standard suite of CLI tools: openapi-generator (generate stubs/clients in 50+ targets); oapi-codegen (Go server + client from OpenAPI); orval (TS clients with axios, fetch, react-query, zod support); oazapfts (minimal TS fetch client); datamodel-code-generator (OpenAPI → Pydantic / dataclasses); spectral (spec linter with custom rulesets); oasdiff (breaking change detector); prism (mock server + contract validation proxy); dredd (spec-vs-implementation contract test runner); schemathesis (property-based testing from OpenAPI); buf (Protobuf-equivalent for gRPC).

section.name: saas-platforms-for-contract-management
section.p: Stoplight (SaaS + desktop) provides visual design + governance with Spectral CLI integration. SwaggerHub (SaaS) offers hosted spec governance + style guides via REST API. Bump.sh (SaaS) provides versioned hosting + breaking-change diff visualization. Apicurio Registry (OSS) is a self-hosted spec/schema registry. Postman (SaaS) enables spec import + contract tests in CI with Newman CLI. Speakeasy (SaaS) generates polished SDK generation across languages. Fern (SaaS + OSS CLI) provides spec-first SDK + docs generation. Buf Schema Registry (SaaS) is the gRPC-equivalent of SwaggerHub. Konnect (Kong, SaaS) enables spec-first deploy to gateway.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: The spec is the source of truth. Hand-edits to generated files fail CI. Lint with a strict Spectral ruleset (operation IDs, descriptions, examples, tags) — agents will skip these otherwise. Run oasdiff breaking on every PR; require a MAJOR bump on any breaking change. Keep the spec under api/openapi.yaml (or split with $ref) and version-control it next to the code; PRs that change behavior must change the spec. Generate at build time, not at commit time — committed generated code is fine, but it must match the spec exactly (CI re-generates and diffs). Provide examples (≥1 per operation, both request and response) — they double as fixture data and improve LLM SDK output. Run schemathesis or dredd in CI: property-based tests catch generator bugs and implementation drift. For events / async, use AsyncAPI 3.0 with @asyncapi/cli; treat the same way as OpenAPI. For gRPC, use buf and the same workflow: spec → generate → impl → contract test.

template.purpose: Minimal OpenAPI 3.1 spec with one POST endpoint, request/response schemas, and error ref
GitHub Actions workflow: lint spec, generate server, compare models, run contract tests
